text: The robotic camera provides a transabdominal view of the inguinal region, displaying the spermatic and epigastric vessels, external iliac vessels, umbilical ligaments, and vas deferens. A bladder catheter is visible, distending the bladder.

The surgeon begins the preperitoneal dissection at the umbilical ligament using electrocautery to achieve hemostasis. A peritoneal incision is made, initially entering the pre-transversalis plane, which is then corrected. The dissection progresses medially to locate the pubis and then laterally, revealing the intermediate fascia. The fascia is reflected up towards the vessels and nerves on the abdominal sidewall and down medially to protect the bladder.

The surgeon begins isolating the hernia sac, using traction to pull it out through the canal in its anatomical orientation. A combination of blunt dissection and electrocautery is employed to separate the sac from adherent surrounding structures, taking care to avoid damaging nearby vessels.

The surgeon works on developing a retropubic space for mesh placement, reflecting fat down towards the bladder in the parietal compartment. On the contralateral side, the surgeon ensures the space extends at least two centimeters beyond the midline, revealing the obturator internus muscle and lacunar ligament. The medial umbilical ligament is manipulated to lateralize the vas deferens, facilitating parietalization and mesh placement. The surgeon prepares to extend the peritoneal dissection for an appropriate mesh landing zone.

The surgeon works around the triangle of doom, carefully orienting the tips of the needle and scissors away from the iliac vessels to develop a space for mesh placement. The iliopubic tract is visible, and the retropubic dissection is well-defined. The surgeon evaluates the femoral, direct, indirect, and obturator spaces before positioning an extra large mid-weight polypropylene mesh. The mesh is secured to the ipsilateral Cooper's ligament, followed by flap closure, with intra-abdominal pressure reduced to maintain visualization.
